diagrams.title: Entropy Visualization: Measuring Uncertainty
diagrams.description: A visual comparison of low entropy (certain, predictable) versus high entropy (uncertain, random) distributions using bar charts. Shows a fair 6-sided die (high entropy = 2.585 bits) versus a biased die (low entropy), with the entropy formula H(X) = -Σ p(x)log₂ p(x) displayed.
elements: Two bar chart groups: uniform distribution (equal bars) and skewed distribution (one dominant bar)
Formula annotation: H(X) = -Σᵢ p(xᵢ) log₂ p(xᵢ)
Bit counter labels under each distribution
Visual metaphor: scattered balls in boxes (ordered vs random)
diagrams.purpose: Introduces entropy as a measure of uncertainty/information content. Helps students understand why uniform distributions have maximum entropy and how entropy quantifies the 'surprise' of an outcome.

diagrams.title: Mutual Information: Venn Diagram Representation
diagrams.description: A classic Venn diagram showing the relationship between entropy of X, entropy of Y, joint entropy, and mutual information. Also includes a scatter plot example showing positive correlation vs zero correlation.
elements: Two overlapping circles labeled H(X) and H(Y)
Central overlap region labeled I(X;Y) = H(X) + H(Y) - H(X,Y)
Outer regions showing unique entropy of each variable
Two side scatter plots: correlated points (elliptical cloud) and uncorrelated points (circular cloud)
Formula annotations for joint and conditional entropy
diagrams.purpose: Explains mutual information as the reduction in uncertainty about one variable given knowledge of another. The Venn diagram visually shows I(X;Y) as the overlap of information shared between variables.

diagrams.title: KL Divergence: Distribution Comparison
diagrams.description: Shows two probability distributions (P and Q) as overlapping area plots/histograms, illustrating how KL divergence measures the 'distance' or information loss when using Q to approximate P. Shows the divergence formula and visual shading of the difference.
elements: Two overlapping bell curves or histograms (P in blue, Q in orange)
Shaded region between curves representing the divergence
Arrow indicating direction: from Q (approximation) to P (true distribution)
Side note showing non-symmetric property: DKL(P||Q) ≠ DKL(Q||P)
diagrams.purpose: Demonstrates KL divergence as a measure of how one distribution differs from another. Critical for understanding likelihood, information theory in compression, and variational inference in machine learning.
